narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can accept right now—it's not about you as a person, but the story your profile tells when stacked against patterns from thousands of others. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, the lowest rung in your school, which screamed the loudest signal of struggle ahead, pulling hardest toward the risk of not passing the bar. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, showing that even early on, grades weren't climbing as they do for those who typically make it through. Your high family income, while a strength in many ways, oddly factored against you here, as the model links it to higher pressure or mismatched preparation in cases like yours. Being Black also nudged the prediction toward failure, reflecting tough broader trends we've seen play out before. Your LSAT of 36.0, solid but not standout, added to the caution, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, which didn't signal the academic firepower needed for bar success. Going full-time and being male rounded out the picture, each tilting things a bit more toward that 43.9% failure risk. Together, these painted a profile where early and late academic deciles dominated the concern, like warning lights flashing about sustaining the grind through law school and beyond. It's a tough combo, but here's the hopeful part: you're not defined by these numbers—you've got a high LSAT that shows real potential, and with targeted steps like boosting study habits or seeking mentorship, you could rewrite this story. Many have turned similar profiles around. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
